description: Explains how Requests models user requests, prepared requests, encoded bodies, hooks, cookies, and response content/status helpers in the lower-level model layer.
document:
The request and response model layer in src/requests/models.py is the bridge between the friendly public API and the concrete HTTP messages handled by sessions and adapters. The API documentation exposes Request and Response as lower-level classes, with PreparedRequest documented one level lower because it represents the finalized outbound message. The quickstart examples show the same split from the user side: simple calls produce a response object, while advanced request inputs such as query parameters, form bodies, JSON bodies, file uploads, streaming, and status checks are all expressed through these model objects.

Request is the user-created container. It records the method, URL, headers, files, body data, query parameters, authentication, cookies, hooks, and optional JSON payload, normalizing missing mapping-like inputs to empty containers. Its main job is not to send anything; it builds a PreparedRequest. During construction it starts with default hook storage and registers any supplied callbacks through RequestHooksMixin, which only accepts supported hook events and callable hook values. Tests around prepared hooks show why this matters: a hook attached to a request is carried into the prepared object and can later alter the response returned by a session.

PreparedRequest is the mutable object containing the exact request that will be sent. Its preparation order is meaningful. The method is uppercased, the URL is converted from bytes or other string-like objects, leading whitespace is removed, HTTP URLs are validated, international hostnames are encoded, and query parameters are appended before any fragment. Headers are stored in a case-insensitive dictionary after validation. Cookies are converted to a cookie jar and used to generate a Cookie header; because the standard cookie machinery will not regenerate that header once present, the cookie preparation step is effectively a one-time operation unless the header is removed.

Body preparation chooses one path from the provided inputs. A JSON body is used only when ordinary data is absent and a JSON value was supplied; it is serialized as UTF-8 bytes, rejects non-compliant numeric values, and adds the JSON content type when the caller has not already supplied one. Mapping or tuple data is form-encoded, while strings, bytes, and readable objects can be sent more directly. File uploads are converted to multipart form data, including regular form fields and file entries with optional filename, content type, and per-part headers. Streamed bodies are treated separately from multipart files: the prepared object records the current body position when possible so redirects can rewind it, uses a known length when available, and otherwise marks the upload for chunked transfer.

Content length handling is deliberately conservative. Tests confirm that bodyless GET and HEAD requests do not receive a length header, while methods that can carry a body receive a zero length when no body is provided. If the caller supplied a length header, Requests preserves it. The tests also cover multivalue form encoding, byte-preserving body handling, UTF-8 byte counts for string data, JSON not overriding explicit data, and the ability to pickle prepared requests while retaining URL, headers, body, hooks, and cookies.

Response represents what came back from the server and keeps a reference to the PreparedRequest that produced it. It stores status, case-insensitive headers, final URL, redirect history, encoding, reason phrase, cookies, elapsed time, and the raw stream. Response.iter_content is the preferred streaming helper because it reads from urllib3 streams or file-like objects, validates the chunk size, decodes compressed transfer encodings through urllib3, and wraps lower-level protocol, decoding, timeout, and SSL failures in Requests exceptions. Response.iter_lines builds on that iterator and is documented as not reentrant safe.

The response body helpers cache and decode content. The byte content property consumes the stream once and returns None when there is no raw response to read. The text view uses the response encoding when available, otherwise falling back to apparent encoding detection. The JSON helper first tries to infer a Unicode encoding for non-empty byte content without an explicit encoding, then falls back through text, always wrapping decoder failures in Requests' JSON decode exception. Status convenience is separate from body decoding: a response is truthy when raise_for_status would not raise, and that method raises an HTTP error only for client or server error ranges.
